meta.name: Ragù alla Bolognese (Classic Meat Ragù)
meta.subtitle: Slow-simmered meat sauce for tagliatelle or lasagna (not a quick tomato sauce)

source.name: ssa-core
notes: Canonical-style ragù: soffritto + meat + small tomato + dairy/wine, gently simmered until rich.
Best with tagliatelle, pappardelle, or layered into lasagna.

yield.notes: Makes ~8 cups sauce (enough for 2 lb pasta or a large lasagna).

notes: Flavor improves after a rest overnight.
Ideal for make-ahead meal prep and freezing in portions.
This is meat-forward; tomato is supporting, not dominant.
Milk smooths acidity and rounds the sauce.

optional.label: Food processor
optional.notes: Speeds up soffritto prep; avoid pureeing too fine.

ingredients.label: Unsalted butter
ingredients.notes: Traditional richness; olive oil + butter is classic.

ingredients.label: Ground pork
ingredients.notes: About 3/4 lb. If skipping, increase beef to 2.25 lb total.

ingredients.label: Pancetta, finely chopped (optional)
ingredients.notes: Adds depth; can omit for a simpler sauce.

ingredients.label: Dry white wine
ingredients.notes: About 1 cup. Dry red also works.

ingredients.label: Beef or chicken stock (as needed)
ingredients.notes: Used to loosen during simmer if it tightens too much.

steps.title: Build the soffritto (gentle, sweet, not browned)
instructions: Heat olive oil and butter in a Dutch oven over medium-low.
Add onion, carrot, and celery with a pinch of salt.
Cook 10–15 minutes, stirring often, until soft and sweet (no strong browning).
checks: Vegetables are very soft and aromatic.
No burnt bits; color should be pale-golden at most.

steps.title: Render pancetta (optional)
instructions: If using pancetta, add it to the pot and cook 3–5 minutes until it releases fat and turns lightly golden.
checks: Pancetta is lightly golden; fat is rendered.

steps.title: Brown the meat properly
instructions: Increase heat to medium.
Add ground beef (and pork if using). Break it up and spread it out.
Cook until most moisture evaporates and the meat browns (10–15 minutes).
Season with black pepper and a bit more salt.
checks: Meat is browned in spots (not gray/steamed).
Fond is forming lightly on the bottom.

steps.title: Deglaze with wine
instructions: Pour in the wine and scrape up any browned bits.
Simmer until the wine is almost fully evaporated and the smell of alcohol is gone (3–6 minutes).
checks: Liquid is reduced; aroma is mellow (not sharp/alcoholic).

steps.title: Add milk (stage 1) to soften and enrich
instructions: Stir in about half the milk (3/4 cup).
Simmer gently, stirring occasionally, until mostly absorbed/evaporated (8–12 minutes).
Add nutmeg (optional) and bay leaf (optional).
checks: Milk is mostly absorbed; sauce looks creamy, not watery.

steps.title: Add tomato and begin the long simmer
instructions: Stir in tomato paste and cook 1 minute to bloom.
Add crushed tomatoes/passata and 1/2 cup water or stock (as needed) to keep it gently simmering.
Bring to a very low simmer (bare bubbles).
checks: Sauce is loose enough to simmer gently, not thick like paste.

steps.title: Simmer low and slow (the flavor-making phase)
instructions: Simmer partially covered for 2–3 hours, stirring every 15–20 minutes.
If it tightens too much, add splashes of stock/water to maintain a gentle simmer.
In the last 30 minutes, stir in the remaining milk (3/4 cup) in 2 additions, letting it absorb between additions.
checks: Sauce is deeply flavored and cohesive; fat is integrated, not greasy.
Texture is thick, spoon-coating, and meat is tender.

steps.title: Final seasoning and serving
instructions: Remove bay leaf (if used).
Taste and adjust salt and pepper.
Serve with tagliatelle/pappardelle or use as lasagna ragù; finish with Parmigiano.
checks: Balance is savory and rich; tomato is present but not dominant.

notes: Cool sauce quickly before refrigerating: portion into shallow containers, refrigerate within 2 hours.
Reheat to steaming hot (74°C / 165°F) before serving.
Refrigerate up to 4 days. Flavor improves overnight.
Freeze up to 3 months in 1–2 cup portions.
Thaw overnight in fridge for best texture.
Reheat gently on stovetop; add a splash of water/stock if needed.
If serving with pasta, loosen with a bit of pasta water for a glossy finish.
Tagliatelle is classic for Bologna-style ragù.
Use as the meat layer sauce for lasagna.
Simple green salad with lemon/olive oil.
Roasted vegetables (broccoli, zucchini, Brussels).
Key success: low simmer + time. Don’t rush it.
Meat must brown (evaporate moisture first) to avoid a flat sauce.
Milk stages make it silky and round; it’s not optional for the classic profile (but can be adapted).
Use 2.25 lb (1020 g) ground beef total and add 1 tbsp olive oil if the pot seems dry during browning.
Dry red adds deeper tannic notes; simmer off alcohol fully before proceeding.
Simmer 75–90 minutes instead of 2–3 hours. Flavor will be lighter but still rich.
Keep the heat very low; prioritize browning and reduction steps.
Best made 1 day ahead; chill overnight to deepen flavor.
Freeze in portions for quick weeknight pasta.
Portion and label with date; keep 1–2 cup increments for flexibility.
